caption: Graphic shows common (red) and less common (yellow) sites of Charcot joints. Etiology is suggested by location.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 1; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Sagittal T1WI MR, part of a series in a patient with diabetic Charcot foot, shows numerous abnormalities. Charcot joints are noted at navicular cuneiforms . There is ↓ signal in the plantar soft tissues, with confluent ↓ signal in the adjacent cuboid , concerning for osteomyelitis. It also shows diffuse ↓ signals throughout the talus , an unexpected finding.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 2; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Sagittal STIR MR in the same patient further defines the talar abnormality as an insufficiency fracture of the dome .

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 3; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Sagittal T1WI C+ FS MR, adjacent slice, shows a wedge-shaped region of hypointensity , representing a segment of osteonecrosis (ON) in the body of the talus. Remember that the talus is prone to ON and that the tarsal bones are prone to insufficiency fracture in diabetic feet. Sinus track led to the cuboid, confirming osteomyelitis, which was suspected based on both T1 and STIR.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 4; archive duplicate STATdx image 22; select STATdx image 21 as a nonduplicate adjacent-slice supplement.

caption: AP radiograph shows swelling about the tarsometatarsal joints . Careful observation reveals fragmentation at the base of the 3rd metatarsal, with likely fragmentation of the bases of the 4th and 5th as well. There was no history of trauma; this is a Charcot foot.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 5 and select STATdx diabetic foot alternatives for recognition reinforcement.

caption: Short-axis CT, same patient, proves the fragmentation and mild subluxation of the metatarsal bases ; it is often far more severe than suspected on the basis of radiographs. As one would expect, the patient is diabetic.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 6 and select distinct STATdx CT views for modality reinforcement.

caption: Lateral radiograph shows an old and healed calcaneal insufficiency avulsion fracture , typical in diabetics. The talonavicular joint shows concavity of the articular surface of the talus, along with some sclerosis , suspicious for early Charcot joint.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 7; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Lateral radiograph of the same foot 2 months later shows ongoing destruction at the talonavicular joint . Debris has migrated dorsal to the talus, contained within a distended joint capsule . This is a classic Charcot joint.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 8; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Sagittal T1WI C+ FS MR in a Charcot foot shows fluid collections (some containing debris) surrounding the ankle and abnormal signal within the distal tibia . Metallic artifact obscures the subtalar, talonavicular, and calcaneocuboid joints.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep; no STATdx duplicate or equivalent fluid-collection/biopsy-negative MR example.

caption: Axial T1WI C+ FS MR in the same patient shows fluid collections in the soft tissues and tendon sheaths . These are neuropathic fluid collections. Bones enhance, related to reactive change. There was no infection at biopsy.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep; no STATdx duplicate or equivalent axial MR tendon-sheath/fluid example.

caption: AP radiograph shows a dislocated shoulder with severe fragmentation and bony debris. The placement of the debris suggests massive distension of the glenohumeral joint and subacromial/subdeltoid bursa . The combination of findings is typical of Charcot (neuropathic) shoulder.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 11; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Sagittal T2 MR, same patient, shows a large cervicothoracic syrinx . Syringomyelia is the most common etiology of Charcot changes in the glenohumeral joint.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 12; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Lateral radiograph of a paraplegic shows a burst fracture of L1 treated with partial corpectomy, strut graft , and posterior rods. Note the osseous destruction and instability at the next 2 levels . These Charcot vertebrae are typically located immediately caudad to the stabilized spine.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 13; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Lateral radiograph shows significant subluxation and destruction of endplates with osseous debris at 2 adjacent levels . This paraplegic patient developed instability and Charcot spine.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 14; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: AP radiograph shows ragged destructive changes of the femoral condyle and osseous debris in a young patient. His contralateral AP knee had similar abnormalities.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 15; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Axial T2WI MR in the same patient shows a large effusion and 1 of multiple pieces of osteocartilaginous debris . This patient's knees show the debris, distension, and destruction typical of neuropathic joints. The etiology in this case is congenital insensitivity to pain.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 16; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: AP radiograph shows an unusual case of neuropathic joint, resulting from congenital indifference to pain. The left knee shows severe disruption with subluxation, fragmentation, attempted repair, and complete destruction of the joint.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep both. Same disease/etiology concept but visually distinct knee examples; not the same screenshot.

caption: Lateral radiograph of contralateral limb in same patient shows just as severe disruption with destruction of the tibiotalar, subtalar, and midfoot joints. Congenital indifference to pain is an unusual cause of Charcot joint and is often polyarticular.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 18; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: AP radiograph in a middle-aged patient gives the initial impression of uncomplicated osteoarthritis, with cartilage loss , large subchondral cyst formation , superolateral subluxation of the hip, and calcar buttressing .

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 19; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Coronal CT, obtained 2 months later in the same patient, shows severe and rapid progression of destruction of the femoral head and acetabulum. Abundant debris is seen within the joint.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 20; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: AP radiograph in the same patient 7 months later shows the classic hatchet sign of rapid destruction of the hip. The rapidity of the destruction and the appearance suggest a diagnosis of rapidly destructive osteoarthritis of the hip. However, one must also consider Charcot arthropathy as an etiology.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep RadPrimer image 21; archive STATdx duplicate.

caption: Axial CT from the same patient shows a small, nodular liver, typical of cirrhosis. The most common etiology of Charcot hip is alcoholism; the cirrhosis proves the etiology in this case.

statdxCoverage.action: Keep; STATdx does not duplicate the cirrhosis/etiology CT context image.

caption: AP view of hypertrophic Charcot arthropathy of the foot in a middle-aged woman with chronic foot swelling and deformity shows lateral subluxation at the 2nd-5th tarsometatarsal (TMT) joints and chronic-appearing smooth periosteal reaction . An old healed 2nd metatarsal neck fracture is also present.
Weight-bearing lateral view in the same patient shows hypertrophic changes at the Lisfranc joint with flattened arch and likely tarsal joint destruction .
Reformatted long-axis NECT in the same patient shows prominent erosion-like changes and bone production involving the 2nd-5th TMT joints with subluxation, consistent with Charcot arthropathy.
Sagittal NECT in the same patient shows the advanced degree of joint destruction at the navicular-medial cuneiform articulation to greater detail, as compared to the radiographs.

AP view shows a dislocated shoulder with severe fragmentation and resorption of the humeral head with bone debris. The placement of the debris suggests massive distention of the glenohumeral joint and subacromial-subdeltoid bursa . The combination of findings is typical of Charcot (neuropathic) shoulder.
duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: Sagittal T2 MR of the cervical spine in the same patient shows a large cervicothoracic syrinx . Syringomyelia is the most common etiology of Charcot changes in the glenohumeral joint.
duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: AP view of a hypertrophic neuropathic knee in a 22-year-old with congenital insensitivity to pain showing the classic Ds of density , dislocation, and debris is shown. Lack of sensation (neuropathy) would be needed to continue walking on this knee to cause this destruction.

Lateral radiograph of the ankle in a patient with congenital indifference to pain shows severe atrophic Charcot arthropathy changes of the ankle and hindfoot. Multiple joints often are involved in these patients.
duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: Lateral view of the thoracolumbar spine in a paraplegic patient shows a burst fracture of L1 treated with partial corpectomy, strut graft , and posterior rods. Note the bone destruction and instability at the next 2 levels , consistent with spinal neuropathic arthropathy.

caption: Lateral view of the thoracolumbar spine shows significant subluxation and destruction of endplates with bone debris at 2 adjacent levels . This paraplegic patient developed instability and spinal neuroarthropathy.
duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: AP view of the pelvis shows destruction of the left hip , typical of a Charcot joint, in this case from tabes dorsalis. The multiple foci of metallic density within the soft tissues of the buttocks were from heavy metal injections for the treatment of syphilis, a historic treatment.

AP view of the hip in a patient with syphilis shows femoral shaft fracture, dislocation of the femoral head, and joint destruction with scattered debris about the joint. This is typical of a Charcot joint at the hip.

Lateral view shows a healed calcaneus insufficiency avulsion fracture , typical in diabetic patients. The talonavicular joint shows concavity of the articular surface of the talus, along with some sclerosis , suspicious for early Charcot joint.
duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: Lateral view in the same patient 2 months later shows ongoing destruction at the talonavicular joint . Debris has migrated dorsal to the talus, contained within a distended joint capsule . This is classic Charcot arthropathy progression.
duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: PA view of the wrist shows neuropathic carpometacarpal joints . There is vascular calcification . This is carpal diabetes-related Charcot arthropathy.

Coronal T1 MR in a patient with Charcot shoulder shows a dislocated and destroyed humeral head surrounded by fluid in the distended joint . The fluid communicates between the glenohumeral joint and subdeltoid bursa through a rotator cuff tear.

Sagittal T1 MR in a patient with diabetic Charcot foot shows numerous abnormalities. There is dislocation of the naviculocuneiform joints . There is decreased signal in the plantar aspect of the cuboid with confluent fat replacement under the cuboid underlying an ulcer. There is diffuse decreased signal throughout the talus .
duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: Sagittal STIR MR in the same patient shows the talus abnormality as an insufficiency fracture of the dome . There is mild edema in the cuboid .
duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: Sagittal T1 C+ FS MR in the same patient shows the talus fracture . It also shows a sinus track extending from the skin ulcer to the cuboid , confirming osteomyelitis there. Note the enhancement of the cuboid .
Sagittal T1 C+ FS MR, adjacent image in the same patient, shows a wedge-shaped region of lack of enhancement or segment of osteonecrosis at the talus.

duplicateEvidence: Classified from staged image_evidence side-by-side visual inspection; same image/slice/screenshot as the selected RadPrimer image.

caption: AP radiograph of the shoulder shows destructive changes within the glenohumeral joint, resulting in a straight cut-off appearance of the humeral head . The humerus is dislocated from the glenoid. There is a huge effusion with debris located within it in the subscapularis recess . These are typical findings of a Charcot shoulder. The patient proved to have syringomyelia.

Lateral radiograph of the ankle shows a long-term Charcot joint involving both the hindfoot and midfoot. The talus has completely resorbed in the process, leaving the tibia articulating with the morphologically abnormal calcaneus. Additionally, the navicular is without proximal articulation, and the calcaneus is significantly subluxated relative to the cuboid.
AP radiograph of the foot in the same patient shows the severe destructive change of the hindfoot and midfoot. This patient also has a plantar-lateral ulceration, which does not approach bone.
AP radiograph of the foot in a patient with peripheral insensate neuropathy shows a classic Charcot foot involving the talonavicular and calcaneocuboid joints.

Axial CT in a patient with Charcot shoulder shows an enormous effusion extending from the shoulder down around the proximal shaft of the humerus . The fluid contains abundant bone debris and could be mistaken for mineralized soft tissue masses.

Sagittal radiograph shows Charcot osteoarthropathy with disorganization of the Chopart (hindfoot-midfoot) joint . The patient is diabetic, and little debris is visible in this atrophic joint. There was a clinically unsuspected subacute fibula fracture as well.

AP radiograph of the knee shows fragmentation and a tense effusion, which decompresses down fascial planes of the leg. This decompression carries bony debris distal to the joint . This is a typical Charcot knee in a patient with acquired syphilis.

AP radiograph of the foot shows lateral dislocation of the metatarsals relative to the tarsals. The medial cortex of the 1st metatarsal should align perfectly with the medial cortex of the medial cuneiform. Vascular calcification is present. This is a Lisfranc dislocation resulting from diabetic neuropathy.
Lateral radiograph of the foot shows diffuse soft tissue swelling about the metatarsals and dorsal subluxation of one of the bases . This should raise suspicion for Charcot joint.

Sagittal CT in the same patient shows fragmentation at the bases of multiple metatarsals and cuboid. This patient was diabetic, and the images demonstrate classic findings and location of diabetic Charcot joint.
